topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white walls of the clinic felt suffocating. I clutched the worn strap of my messenger bag, the fabric digging into my shoulder. Dr. Elias Thorne sat across from me, his expression a practiced calm that grated on my nerves like sandpaper. He had the same sharp, assessing eyes I remembered from the schoolyard, only now they held a disconcerting depth. Every time he leaned forward to make a point, a familiar tightening gripped my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. "Tell me again about the incident with the locker," he prompted, his voice smooth, devoid of any of the usual sneer. I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry. The memory of his mocking laughter, the way he’d deliberately tripped me, rushed back, leaving a cold slickness behind my eyes. I stared at the floor tiles, focusing intently on a tiny crack, desperate to avoid meeting his gaze.

[story 2]

It started with a mandatory referral. My mother, exhausted by my escalating anxiety, had suggested therapy. I resisted, a knot forming in my stomach that felt too large to ignore. But the alternative—the silence at home—was worse. When I walked into the office, the air immediately shifted. There he was, impeccably dressed, radiating an authority that used to make me shrink. He offered me a chair, and I sat down rigidly, my hands gripping my knees so tightly my knuckles turned white. "Welcome," he said, his tone neutral, yet somehow laced with an undercurrent of something I couldn't quite decipher—recognition? I kept my eyes fixed on the knot of his tie, unwilling to let my gaze drift to the familiar set of his jaw. The silence stretched, heavy and charged, punctuated only by the faint hum of the ventilation system.

[story 3]

I paced the length of the waiting room, the polished floor reflecting my agitated movements. Each footfall seemed amplified in the quiet space, a drumbeat against the rising pressure inside me. When the door finally opened, I stopped dead. He was there, not the swaggering, cruel figure from my youth, but a man in tailored charcoal, radiating an unnerving stillness. He offered a small, almost apologetic nod. "Come in," he murmured. As I approached his desk, I could feel the residue of years—the sharp edges of old resentments—still clinging to the atmosphere. I tried to keep my voice steady, but it came out thin, reedy. "I don't know why I'm here," I managed, the words tasting like ash. He simply watched me, his gaze steady, and for a fleeting second, I saw something in those eyes that mirrored the judgment I used to inflict.
